snippet:
from collections import defaultdict
words = ["apple", "banana", "avocado", "cherry", "blueberry"]
groups = defaultdict(list)
for i in range(len(words)):
    w = words[i]
    groups[w[0]].append(w)
[groups[k] for k in sorted(groups)]
cue: Bucket each word under its first character, then read groups in key order.
Split the numbers into even and odd buckets keyed by the remainder.

prompt: Write `is_anagram(s, t)` that returns True if `t` is a rearrangement of the characters of `s`, else False. Compare character counts.
cue: Two strings are anagrams exactly when their character tallies match.

concept: build a dict from paired lists (zip truncates)
cue: zip pairs the lists position by position; dict turns those pairs into entries.
note: zip stops at the shorter input, so the third key with no matching value is silently dropped. This truncation is a common source of quietly-missing entries.

cue: Bare split() treats any run of whitespace as one separator.
note: split() with no argument splits on any whitespace run and drops leading, trailing, and empty pieces. split(' ') with an explicit space does NOT: it keeps the empty strings between doubled spaces.

prompt: Write `word_count(text)` that returns a dict mapping each whitespace-separated word in `text` to how many times it appears. Do not use collections.Counter.
cue: Split into words, then tally each by index with a miss-safe read.
mapsTo: pattern behind LC 819 · Most Common Word

prompt: Write `majority(nums)` that returns the value appearing more than `len(nums) // 2` times. Assume such an element always exists.
cue: Count every value, then take the single most frequent one.

prompt: Write `top_k_frequent(nums, k)` that returns the `k` most frequent values in `nums`, most frequent first.
cue: Count all values, then take the k highest-count pairs and keep only the keys.
note: Counter.most_common does the ranking for you; done by hand the interview-optimal way is bucket sort by frequency (O(n)) or a size-k heap (O(n log k)).

snippet:
nums = [3, 1, 2, 4]
prefix = 0
sums = []
for i in range(len(nums)):
    prefix += nums[i]
    sums.append(prefix)
sums
cue: Keep one running total; after each element, record the sum so far.
note: sums[j] minus sums[i] is the sum of the slice between i and j. The next exercise counts subarrays with this idea.

snippet:
nums = [1, 1, 1]
k = 2
count = {0: 1}
prefix = 0
total = 0
for i in range(len(nums)):
    prefix += nums[i]
    total += count.get(prefix - k, 0)
    count[prefix] = count.get(prefix, 0) + 1
total
cue: Track how often each running total has occurred; a matching earlier total means a subarray sums to k.
note: prefix[j] - prefix[i] == k means the slice (i, j] sums to k, so each earlier prefix equal to prefix - k is one more valid subarray.

concept: count subarrays summing to k (prefix-sum map)
prompt: Write `subarray_sum(nums, k)` that returns the number of contiguous subarrays of `nums` whose elements sum to `k`. Values may be negative. Make one pass using a dict of prefix-sum counts.
cue: Keep a running prefix sum; for each one, add how many earlier prefixes equal prefix - k.
note: Seed {0: 1} so a prefix that itself equals k counts on its own; negatives break the sliding-window trick, so the prefix map is the fallback.

snippet:
nums = [100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2]
s = set(nums)
starts = [x for x in sorted(s) if x - 1 not in s]
starts
cue: A value begins a consecutive run only when its predecessor is missing from the set.
note: Counting a run only from its start visits each element once, giving O(n) instead of the O(n log n) you'd pay to sort.

concept: longest consecutive run via a set
prompt: Write `longest_consecutive(nums)` that returns the length of the longest run of consecutive integers in `nums` (in any order). Return 0 for an empty list. Do not sort; use a set.
cue: From each run-start, walk upward through the set counting how far the run extends.
